sentences.text: Between 13 and 15 months, somewhere-- 13, 14 months.
Really, all of 1943 was like nothing happened.
I mean, after my father passed away, we buried him.
And everybody-- we were just really worried the first few months he was going to keep us or not keep us.
And then it was, like, almost routine.
We went out once a week to get food.
And all day long we, we, we kept quiet.
I'm sure the, the cows downstairs had more room than we had.
But we didn't-- and then we started-- we were-- towards the end of '43 was, you know, the radios for what's happening, how far the Germans were.
They went to Russia to Stalingrad, and [INAUDIBLE] almost to Moscow, like 18 kilometers or something before Moscow.
And then when they-- then they started, the Russians started advancing towards us.
We, we did not have a radio.
But he used to come in like every day, every day and say-- and, and, bring us, bring us up to date.
He was a very intelligent man.
Very-- he was so very intelligent.
And he was a religious guy, but a very modern religion.
You know, it's-- he used to came up, come up and, and give us like, you know, they, they did, they did not conquer Moscow.
They did not [INAUDIBLE].
And then-- and the second or the third-- the last quarter of 1943, the Russians started moving, moving up, and the Germans started, you know, started retreating.
So by, by November, October, November, it was, you know, it was the when, when, when, and when, you know, and all the stuff.
When will they come?
And it-- so '43 was like a dead issue.
I mean, it was like, you live like an animal.
You didn't know anything else.
It's just survival, you know, sleep, get up, and then, you know-- and then, when it came to New Years of the end of '43, [INAUDIBLE] was a lot of noise.
The Russians are coming back.
They're coming back.
And the-- But then for some reason they stopped, you know, and it took, like-- but finally in February, they, they passed us through.
And they stopped.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they stopped in that little village where we used to get our food.
And the minute they got there, we, we all went, picked up, and went to that village.
He told us.
I mean, the guy-- the Russians are here, the-- they're here.
And they're, and they're right there, and they're-- and the army's there, and the whole thing.
So we picked up, and we went to the guy that used to give us the food.
And they were right in the same town.
In fact, they were in his house.
Because they, they took his house for some command or something.
And they treated us very good.
Can't complain, and we knew they were-- they had to leave, too, because it was the front line.
And they gave us an escort at that time and took us to Luck, to that city.
And when we got to Luck--
No, they put us-- they gave us transportation with horses.
And they took us to Luck.
When we got to Luck, there was some semblance of our Jewish organization.
They, they put you up, you know.
And we went to their office, and there was a lot of bombed-out places, so they gave us an apartment.
And we stayed there a few weeks.
Some carried some stuff, you know, food that a guy gave us, and very little possessions, very little possessions.
And we got, we got to Luck, and they gave us an apartment, which was more than we had, we had for the last two years.
But the front line started to retreat, and it was very big commotions.
I mean, are they going retreat behind Luck or they going to hold them?
I was--
Oh, oh, yeah.
There, there was-- you could hear with the fighting going on was just a few kilometers out of Luck.
Because they were afraid-- because the Russians, at that moment, started to retreat again.
After a while, you get so used to it.
And once you know that there's really, it's really [INAUDIBLE], you know.
If it's-- after a while, you get used to-- it sounded like they were already in town.
That's how it sounded.
But they were not, by the way.
But we didn't take any chances.
So most of us, I mean, not only our family, but a lot of Jews, you know, started going through Rowne, because that was further, further away.
It's east.
It's-- it's east.
Well, directly--
From the Nazis--
Russian.
But, yeah.
It was Russian.
We knew they weren't going all the way back to, to Moscow, so we retreated to Rowne.
And we walked all the way, though.
It's got to be 60, 70 kilometers.
Well, partly we walked, and partly the Russian soldiers used to pick us up and drop us off, you know.
It took, it took us three days to get there.
Between riding, it wasn't bad.
It took us three days to get there.
Well, we did really bad, I should say.
But you know, the people along the way, they, they were very good to-- at that time, we were not as Jews already.
We were just retreating because the Germans were coming back.
So they, they'll feed you.
They'll put us up.
People were nice.
They put us up overnight, you know, on the floor.
And the-- all of them suffered, so they-- there was very-- a lot of compassion.
We got, we got to Rowne.
There was-- it, it, it had to be before the, before the war ended, so it had to be something like March.
It was still in '44.
The, the-- March or maybe April of '44.
The-- oh, yeah.
And the, the war was still going on.
I mean, the war was still at the-- when we got to Rowne, we got an apartment somehow there.
We got-- we did get an apartment, and all of us still stayed together, all eight of us.
But what happened while this was going on-- and Rowne is in a key link with-- to supply the front line for the Russians.
So they bombed us, I want you to know, for six weeks, every single night.
That was, that was the worst part probably, as a child, to remember every single night they lit up the skies and bombed us.
In fact, we, we used to run for the basement [INAUDIBLE].
And, and one night-- and we had a church right across the street.
And one night-- initially, a lot of people were killed, because they ran out to the streets.
And they used to get killed from the shrapnels, you know, just from the pieces.
And one night, a bomb came in to part of the church and into the basement we were, but it didn't explode.
And it honestly didn't explode.
Oh, they're monsters.
It didn't explode.
And since then, I want you to know, I never went in another basement.
[INAUDIBLE].
Used to sleep upstairs.
The windows, the windows come out.
And this lasted about six weeks.
Stay upstairs because it doesn't pay.
They said-- after a while, it's really-- now, why would you-- OK, get down, get down.
Because me and my cousin, we always talk about-- says, you know, they used to run to this cellar.
We [INAUDIBLE], no more.
Oh, windows were gone.
I mean, like, it's unbelievable.
And the following day, they took it out.
They dismantled it.
[INAUDIBLE] But we had the worst time as the six weeks in Rowne, where the, where the bombing, the German bombing.
And then as soon the front line moved away, it became normal.
I mean, normal that, you know, nobody fired at you.
And they set up the soup kitchens and organized.
I mean, somehow we was organized pretty good.
And maybe there's a black market in [INAUDIBLE], you know, and-- from the, from the Russian soldiers who sold them vodka.
My mother was baking rolls and selling them in the markets to survive.
And my brother went to the army.
He was in the-- he went into the army.
This, this-- he went to the army at the end of '44, so he was, like, 19, I guess.
He wasn't even-- he was, he was born 1925, so he was 19.
He really-- they probably would have drafted him, but he volunteered.
He did volunteer.
But the-- we all thought it was nuts, but he did volunteer.
I think he wanted to get away from all this stuff.
It's like, you know, being, being able to do something and maybe want to get revenge on the Germans.
But I think it's like, you know, being penned up for all these years-- you're talking about from '41-- I mean, now they said it was '44 was three years longer.
I think he just, like, wanted to go out in the world a little bit.
He went all the way to Konigsberg and then was wounded, and they sent him back in-- before the war ended.
But he was wounded.
He had a leg wound.
They sent him back to somewhere past Moscow.
But he went all the way to Germany.
He was wounded, and then we brought him home in '45.
And what I mean by brought him home, because they wouldn't release him so fast, so my mother and my sister made a trip out there, paid somebody off, and got him out.
Because-- in the, the most that we want to get him out because we wanted to leave Russia, and we didn't want to leave him there.
It was at that time, they came out if you were under Polish denomination before '39, and if you wanted to go to Poland, you can do that.
So I wasn't going to go without him, right?
So you fit that category?
You had permission to go back?
Right.
But we had to get in.
So we went out there and--
Oh, yeah, by train.
It's the only way through there.
And they paid off to get him out.
No, he was-- he had some discomfort, you know, after [INAUDIBLE].
It probably wasn't healed right.
But no, he was-- he just, he had a leg wound, you know.
He just-- he was in a hospital, and then he came out.
No.
[INAUDIBLE]
He, he, he had to come back, probably, of June of-- June of '45, something like that, because we waited for him to be able to leave.
Well, it was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] first of all, we never-- we, we told him it was never going to happen.
You can't buy modern Russian army, right?
And-- because first of all, they went on false papers.
My mother--
--and sister, because you could never get permission to go, to go that far.
So they went on false papers completely, you know.
And [INAUDIBLE] figure you're going to get killed.
You're going to get arrested before you get them.
But he came back.
It was, it was really, at that time, you know, like the, the biggest thing happening-- get him out-- for them to come back.
Because, I mean, they had papers.
Somebody picked them up, that was probably 10 years in jail.
That's right.
Because you figure, you know, they pick you up.
I mean, we knew it was false papers.
I mean, there was no question about it, was standing about the--
I was, I was almost 15, yeah.
And they brought him back.
They brought him back.
And it was-- and we didn't really hang out very long.
We got the papers, and we moved.
And we, we moved to, I think-- moved to Bytom.
It's in Poland.
It's B-I-D-O-M.
Oh, yeah, during that time, yeah.
We went back to Ostrozec during that time.
We-- first of all.
I went back to, to Ostrozec twice.
First, I went back to see what happened.
And that was actually before the, before the war, the war ended.
So I went back to see what happened.
And the whole town was dismantled.
I went with my cousin.
First, I went with my cousin.
We were-- yeah.
We went-- in fact, we went to visit a guy that saved us.
It sounds funny.
And he gave us two horses back-- and we went to the town, and then we came back there.
The whole town was dismantled.
Absolutely all the homes--
No, dismantled.
Really, they sold the-- the way I understand it happened, they sold the houses to the farmers, and they dismantled them.
And there must have been around six, seven homes, and our home was there.
And, and, and who was there?
The Russian police.
Living-- not living.
They had it as a, as a police station.
And they, and they let us go in and look around and everything.
But you really couldn't live there because it was like, there's nobody there.
I mean, there was not one person, one Jew there.
I mean, no-- nobody lived there.
And then we--
Was taken apart brick by brick.
Nothing left.
It was really-- it was amazing to see it, like all these wooden homes and, you know-- with straw roofs, and nothing left.
About six homes, five, six homes, that was the whole thing.
No, it was a police station.
The army--
No, there was no army.
It was a police station.
So then you took the horses and you went back to the farmer.
Went back there.
And then, then we went back by-- yeah.
We used to do a lot of hitchhiking.
In 1945, to get from place to place, the trains would take you for the army.
Everything-- you did a lot of hitchhiking, but the, the Russian army, they'll pick you up from place to place.
So we went back there, and then we begged to-- and went to a small place in [NON-ENGLISH].
And then we used to hitchhike, used to hitchhike a lot.
She didn't-- I, I don't think she was real worried about it, you know.
You go through so much that you really don't think the dangers.
They really don't think of it.
But there was a lot of danger.
The war was still going on.
The war was still going on.
But it was so far away.
It was so-- the war was already in Germany.
It was already out of Poland.
It was already in Germany, you know.
First, we really wanted to see what happened.
What happened?
And it's like curiosity.
And is there anybody there?
There was nobody.
There was nobody there.
Yes.
And then we went back a second time.
No, from, from Rowne.
Once we, once we left Rowne, that was it.
Well, we-- I went with my mother at that time.
Yeah, me and my mother went back.
She wanted to see it.
And then we hid some-- before we left, we, we hid some Russian gold rubles in the basement.
And I said to her-- and I, I was the guy that hid it, by the way.
Because I was a very trusted young kid.
So I said, no, we go back, we can take it out.
And she wanted to see-- she, she couldn't believe what happened to Ostrozec.
So we went back, and the police station was still there.
They let us in again.
They were very cordial to us.
We went into the basement, and she chickened out.
She says, you're not going to do that.
She says, you're going to get arrested if you're going to do that.
We didn't do it.
So it's still there.
So it's still there.
It's still there, the, the Russian-- gold and Russian rubles are still there.
And that was the last time I was there.
I did go to-- I did go to Russia in '89, [INAUDIBLE], but I never went all the way there.
I went over to Kiev, but I didn't go any, any further west.
Approximately under 200 miles.
I would say probably 160 miles, something like that.
And she wouldn't let--
She wouldn't dig up the gold.
She wouldn't let me do that.
I went back to Rowne and--
And then we left.
We went to Bytom, so that was already in '45, and the war was already ended.
That was-- it was-- I believe we got there, it was in June.
The war was already ended, and they were very well organized, you know.
The, the [NON-ENGLISH] was there, we know, and not officially.
They're very organized, and they really shuffled people where to go.
And we, we stayed there a short period of time, probably, maybe two months, maybe not.
And we-- they used to shuffle from place to place, like we went to Czechoslovakia first.
And then we, we supposedly went as Greek-- Greeks coming home.
It was really, you know, like-- and then from there, we went to Czechoslovakia, and then we went to Austria, and from Austria, we want to Munich.
All-- they were leaving.
I mean, we were just like a cow being led, you know.
They, they-- everything was arranged.
And there was the [NON-ENGLISH], the Jewish organization that officially, obviously, they couldn't do that.
And we got all the way to Munich in 1945 before Christmas.
It was Bytom.
I really gave you the wrong name.
[INAUDIBLE]
No, there was no DP camp after that the first camp.
So we got to Munich, and then, and then the-- and it was really a lot of-- a, a lot of Jews came to Munich at the time.
Then when the DP camps were created by the UNNRA, I think, the United-- not the United Nations, United something.
They created UNNRA, U-N-N-R-A.
And the DP camps were created.
First, so in the [INAUDIBLE], we stayed in the, the Munich Museum.
It was bombed down, but we stayed in the Munich Museum.
No, there was no art there.
Big empty rooms.
And the-- in Austria, we stayed in [INAUDIBLE] hospital was half bombed-out, too.
So in Munich, they, they really started resettling the people.
First, they sent us to a small town.
The name was [NON-ENGLISH], but we didn't like it.
We came back and said we'd rather go to the DP camp.
We went to Fohrenwald.
And we stayed in Fohrenwald.
I mean, it was really organized.
I mean, there was enough food.
I mean, there was shelter.
Obviously, it was a barracks before.
And it, it was a big camp.
Fohrenwald was one of the biggest DP camps.
They had Regensburg.
But the-- but Fohrenwald was a big, very well run DP camp.
And people were waiting.
A lot of people went to Israel before Israel became a state.
A lot of people want to Norway.
I mean, they're [INAUDIBLE].
I think it had to be well over 2,000, 3,000 people.
It was all survivors.
All survivors.
In camps, in hiding, all, all people leaving Russia, Poland, and trying to move west.
I mean, all the way, even South America, anyplace.
Everybody was moving someplace.
The people that I, that I, that we associate, nobody wanted to stay there.
But, you know, they were really pretty well-- Munich was bombed out.
And the Germans were really, you know-- you would like to see them struggle, because they really struggled.
I mean, they had no food, and bombed out, and no, and no shelter.

You can see Munich today, but you had to see it in '45 or-- it was like-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we were just hoping that they, they would never fix it up, but obviously the Marshall Plan did a pretty good job on that one.
And then we, and then we were there-- I was there till '47.
And while we're there, we went to school at a Hebrew school, which I have a picture of it.
And it was normal, very normal life.
You know, you went to school.
After school, you had facilities, you know, gymnasiums if you wanted to play ball.
We had a soccer team.
It was very, very, very organized.
But we all know it was temporary.
You know, we got to leave.
Yes, we-- I lived-- my cousin, my cousin was not with us no more.
He, he went to, actually, to a training camp in the area not far away.
He went, he went there.
Yes, 16, that's right.
He went there.
He didn't want to-- he did not come there with us.
My-- the rest of us were together, except, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] except for the, the person that survived with us, which I still can't think of her name right now.
She did not go with us.
She went to a different place, but also to a DP camp.
She also went to the DP camp, but she didn't stay with us.
Yeah, it was normal.
Yeah, it was two years, almost two years.
Yeah.
[INAUDIBLE] It was very normal.
You went to school.
And we did some black market, you know, if you wanted a little luxury.
Which I was arrested, so one weekend I stayed in jail in--
--in Frankfurt, yeah.
Well, we, we smuggled some, believe it or not, it's schmaltz, some fat, you know, from, from Munich to Frankfurt.
And it was very-- so I used to do it on the weekends.
I was picked up.
We bought it in-- we bought it where we were.
And we used to go on the train and go to Frankfurt.
Sell it, yeah.
It was more expensive there.
And also salami, this type.
And it was, you know, to get some luxuries, and-- so one weekend, I, I, I was picked up.
I was with another guy, but I was the only guy picked up, because they just like, you know-- and I spent July 4th weekend in a, in a Frankfurt jail, which was not very pleasant, by the way.
Yeah, that's, that's the point.
Not as a Jew.
It was just black market, yes.
And they--
Jail was terrible.
I mean, I think that's the only time I've ever spent a day, but it's a two by four.
It was with another person there, and I got stuck with July 4th weekend.
The way I got released is they really took me to, to American, not, not to German court.
And I really gave them some story, which really made no sense, and the guy really couldn't care less, and he dismissed the case, you know.
They-- just about getting [INAUDIBLE].
You know what I mean?
Then when I got out, because I was supposed to be back the day before, but they really didn't know when to look for me.
But by the time I got out and I called them, it was-- it was an extra experience which I could have lived without.
No, no.
That was really the end of it.
It was really getting too close for us to leave.
So my cousin-- let's see, he went, he went to go to Israel.
I'll be honest with you, maybe I shouldn't say, but I didn't want to go.
I said, I just have enough of war, and I really don't want to go to Israel right now.
He said, you're, you're crazy.
All these years, we're all together.
I said, Aaron, I really got to hang out longer.
He didn't.
He left before-- he left in '47, and he went to Israel.
And he got to Israel-- different story.
He got to Israel before Israel was a state.
Illegally, yes.
He came illegally, and he's a year older than I am, and he served in every war, '48, '56, '73, and, and '67.
And want you know, he never got a scratch.
Could not believe that.
He was here two years ago, and never got a scratch.
He has-- and now he has, he has three kids.
He lives in a kibbutz, [NON-ENGLISH].
And that's-- he's a very happy guy.
He got off the kibbutz once.
He didn't like it, and he went back.
But none of his kids are in kibbutz anymore.
They're all off the kibbutz.
Well, I've been to Israel probably six, seven times.
The last time I was there was when his youngest son got married.
But I-- he was-- yeah, he was here twice.
I brought him here twice.
Last time I brought him was for my granddaughter's bar mitzvah.
And that's when I made my, my, my bar mitzvah, so I brought him up.
So in '47, we couldn't-- it was getting itchy.
Now, when are we going to get out of Germany?
Got to leave Germany, new [INAUDIBLE] state.
Obviously, everybody wanted to go to America, United States.
It didn't look that we have anybody that would be able to do that.
You, you had an aunt here.
She couldn't help you?
Somehow, I really don't-- either, either they weren't willing-- I really don't want to, you know, say anything.
She's not alive.
May she rest in peace.
Somehow, we didn't get any papers to go.
And there was no-- didn't look like [INAUDIBLE].
You know, we-- sooner or later, after Israel, I guess everybody knows you're going to wind up in Israel.
So when the Canadian Jewish congress came out that you can-- they would pick everybody up, anybody under 18 with no parents.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So it was again in Europe, they have papers, very easy.
No.
It, it was just to get out, you know, get, get out of Germany, get out of Germany.
And got my paper, that I was, you know [INAUDIBLE], and I went, and I went to Canada.
By myself, in, in June of '47.
Oh, it's a lot of people.
And I, I have some of the pictures, too, that I'll show you.
We really had a boat full of people.
I would say a couple thousand.
Everybody under 18.
You had to be under 18.
You [INAUDIBLE].
Yeah, we had a guide.
Yeah, we had a guide who was in charge of the whole, the whole thing.
And, and they split us up into groups, you know, on the boats.
These are going to Montreal.
They're going to Toronto.
They're going to Winnipeg.
You know, it's-- it was [INAUDIBLE].
And somehow I was the one that went to Montreal.
20, 25, maybe.
A, a lot of--
All the same age.
They had to be within, you know, under, under 18.
Maybe a few of them made themselves younger, you know, but there, there were not too many over 20.
It was a pretty good time in Montreal.
I mean, you know, we went-- we went-- I went to work, and I went to night, night school.
They set, they set us up with, with families, with Jewish families.
And they paid for the room.
And they set up each one with, with a different family.
I stayed with, I stayed with a family in-- [INAUDIBLE], I think it was.
And my-- and I stayed there not long, though, really.
I went on my own.
I really didn't want to live this way, so I-- I had a-- one of my friends and myself, we went out on our own, so we moved away from the families.
No.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I thought it was Lieberman [INAUDIBLE].
Probably about four or five months.
And then myself and a friend, we went on our own, so we got our own place, and--
Harry Lieberman.
He was--
